steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which finding would most directly support Mulder and Spellman's conclusion: that invasive species (like P. aviculare) are advantaged by delays in subfreezing temperature onset more than native species (like V. edule). A supporting finding must show a clear differential benefit to the invasive species specifically when onset is late.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Although V. edule and P. aviculare both tended to produce leaves later into autumn in years with late subfreezing temperature onset, the extension was much greater for P. aviculare than for V. edule': This directly shows the invasive gaining more from the delay than the native does — the exact differential advantage the conclusion claims. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Although significant interannual variations in subfreezing temperature onset were observed during the study, neither P. aviculare nor V. edule showed any significant interannual variation in the cessation of leaf production': If neither species responds differently to onset timing, there is no advantage for either — this undermines rather than supports the conclusion. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Although V. edule and P. aviculare tended to stop producing leaves at about the same time in years with historically typical temperature patterns, P. aviculare stopped producing leaves sooner than V. edule did in years with late subfreezing temperature onset': This shows the invasive ending leaf production earlier when onset is late — a disadvantage, not an advantage. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Although V. edule and P. aviculare both tended to produce more leaves overall in years with late subfreezing temperature onset, the years with late subfreezing temperature onset also had early growing season onset in spring': This introduces a spring confound; both species benefit equally and the added variable obscures whether late-onset itself is the cause. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
